items.myth: Self-supervised learning is the same as unsupervised learning.
items.reality: Both use unlabeled raw inputs, but SSL always defines a prediction task with pseudo-labels from data (MLM, next token, contrastive pairs). Classic unsupervised methods like k-means or PCA optimize structure without that explicit surrogate label.
items.example: K-means assigns cluster IDs iteratively but does not frame a fixed input→label pair from each sample's structure the way masking a token does.

items.myth: Self-supervised models never use human labels.
items.reality: SSL refers to how pretraining obtains targets. Almost all deployed models also undergo supervised fine-tuning, RLHF, or evaluation on human-labeled benchmarks for the actual product task.
items.example: BERT is pretrained with MLM (SSL) then fine-tuned on GLUE tasks with human sentiment and NER labels.

items.myth: Any data augmentation automatically makes training self-supervised.
items.reality: Augmentation alone is preprocessing. SSL requires a defined pretext objective where the model predicts something derived from the data — e.g., which augmentation came from the same instance in contrastive learning.
items.example: Random flips during supervised training are augmentation; SimCLR uses two augmented views of the same image as a positive pair with a contrastive loss — that is SSL.
